primary_diagnosis.name: Acute (viral) myocarditis with early left-ventricular dysfunction

pathophysiology_summary: Myocarditis is inflammation of the myocardium, commonly triggered by a viral infection (direct injury plus immune-mediated damage) - here, typically 1-2 weeks after a viral illness. Inflammation of the heart muscle impairs contractility and electrical stability, producing chest pain (which may be pericarditic/positional if there is accompanying pericardial involvement - myopericarditis), a disproportionate resting tachycardia (the heart compensating for reduced stroke volume, out of proportion to the modest fever), progressive dyspnoea and fatigue from early left-ventricular dysfunction, and signs such as an S3 gallop, a borderline-raised JVP and mild dependent oedema. The key distinction from pericarditis is that myocarditis involves the muscle (troponin rise, S3, tachycardia and heart-failure signs) rather than only the lining, and it carries a materially higher risk: myocarditis can progress unpredictably and rapidly to fulminant heart failure, cardiogenic shock, or malignant ventricular arrhythmia and sudden death, which is why continuous cardiac monitoring in hospital is required. Diagnosis rests on troponin (elevated), ECG (non-specific changes, arrhythmia), echocardiography (LV function/effusion) and cardiac MRI (late gadolinium enhancement). Importantly, and in contrast to viral pericarditis, high-dose NSAIDs are relatively contraindicated in myocarditis (associated with worse outcomes in experimental models), and exercise must be strictly restricted. None of the diagnostic tests, monitoring or safe treatment can be delivered over telehealth, so the correct action is urgent hospital assessment with strict rest, not remote NSAIDs or a next-day wait.

differential.evidence_basis: New chest pain, a constantly racing heart and breathlessness a couple of weeks after a viral illness in a young adult is a classic myocarditis pattern.
The positional pain could be pericardial, but disproportionate tachycardia, an S3 and heart-failure signs point to muscle involvement (myocarditis) rather than isolated pericarditis.
Breathlessness and tachycardia warrant considering PE, though the clear viral prodrome, S3 and absence of DVT risk make it less likely.
Chest pain always prompts considering ACS, but the young age, viral prodrome and positional non-crushing pain make it less likely (troponin/ECG still needed).

differential_progression.scoring_note: The AI Doctor should recognise probable myocarditis, escalate for urgent hospital monitoring, and avoid remote NSAIDs and exertion.

differential.diagnosis: Acute myocarditis with early LV dysfunction
differential.evidence_basis: Viral prodrome (DI-001) + disproportionate resting tachycardia (DI-002) + progressive dyspnoea (DI-003) + positional chest ache (DI-004), with (sealed) S3 and borderline raised JVP, indicates myocarditis with early heart-failure signs.
There may be pericardial involvement too (myopericarditis), but the muscle signs dominate; both need assessment, and NSAIDs are avoided when the muscle is involved.
Viral prodrome, S3 and absence of DVT risk make PE unlikely (though excluded formally in hospital).
Young age, viral prodrome and positional pain make ACS unlikely, though troponin/ECG are still done.

differential_progression.scoring_note: After the red-flag screen this is myocarditis with early LV dysfunction needing urgent hospital admission for monitoring, troponin/echo/MRI, strict rest, and NO NSAIDs.

differential.diagnosis: Acute myocarditis with early LV dysfunction - requiring urgent hospital admission for monitoring, troponin, echocardiography and cardiac MRI
differential.evidence_basis: Working diagnosis for telehealth. Confirmation and safe management (monitoring, troponin, echo, MRI) require hospital admission; the correct action is urgent transfer with strict rest.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Final telehealth framing is myocarditis needing urgent hospital assessment with continuous monitoring and strict rest - not remote NSAIDs, exertion or a next-day wait.

red_flags.description: New cardiac symptoms following a recent viral illness (viral prodrome)
red_flags.clinical_significance: A post-viral onset is characteristic of myocarditis and should prompt cardiac assessment rather than dismissal as post-viral fatigue.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Myocarditis dismissed as lingering virus; sudden arrhythmia or fulminant heart failure occurs.

red_flags.description: Disproportionate constant resting tachycardia (~118 bpm) relative to a low-grade temperature
red_flags.clinical_significance: Tachycardia out of proportion to fever suggests the heart muscle is strained and is a key myocarditis clue.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Under-recognition of myocardial strain delays urgent monitoring.

red_flags.description: Progressive dyspnoea and severely reduced exercise tolerance (early heart failure)
red_flags.clinical_significance: Breathlessness at low workload indicates early left-ventricular dysfunction and limited reserve.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Progression to fulminant heart failure without monitoring.

red_flags.description: S3 gallop, borderline raised JVP (4 cm) and mild ankle oedema on clinician examination
red_flags.clinical_significance: These clinician-elicited signs indicate early left-ventricular dysfunction/volume overload from myocarditis; auscultation and JVP assessment cannot be done over telehealth.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Not applicable to telehealth - requires clinician examination; the correct action is urgent hospital assessment.

red_flags.description: Elevated troponin, ECG changes/arrhythmia and late gadolinium enhancement on cardiac MRI - pending
red_flags.clinical_significance: Troponin, ECG and cardiac MRI confirm myocardial injury/inflammation; they cannot be obtained over telehealth.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Not applicable to telehealth - requires inpatient investigation; the correct action is urgent transfer.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: A racing heart and breathlessness after a virus can be myocarditis; attributing them to slow recovery or anxiety misses a dangerous diagnosis.
Unlike pericarditis, NSAIDs are relatively contraindicated in myocarditis; the AI Doctor must not advise ibuprofen and should defer treatment to hospital.
Myocarditis can progress rapidly to fulminant heart failure or lethal arrhythmia; continuous monitoring is required, so urgent transfer (not a next-day wait) is essential.
Exercise worsens myocardial inflammation and arrhythmic risk; strict rest is mandatory.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Cardiac auscultation (S3 gallop) and assessment of JVP
Accurate clinician-measured heart rate, blood pressure and oxygenation
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Urgent hospital assessment (ambulance transfer given the arrhythmia risk and living alone) for continuous cardiac monitoring, troponin, ECG, echocardiography and cardiac MRI; enforce strict bed rest with no exertion, do NOT take high-dose NSAIDs, and call 000 to upgrade if he loses consciousness, develops sudden severe breathlessness, or feels a rapid fluttering in the chest.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Reassuring the patient it is post-viral recovery, advising NSAIDs, or deferring to a few-day GP wait risks sudden fulminant heart failure or fatal arrhythmia without monitoring.
